questions.question: What is the underlay in an SD-Access fabric?
options: The virtual tunnels built on top of the network
The underlying physical network providing IP connectivity
questions.explanation: The underlay is the physical network of devices and wired/wireless connections that provides IP connectivity. The overlay is virtual tunnels on top of it.

questions.question: Which protocol builds the tunnels that form the SD-Access overlay data plane?
questions.explanation: VXLAN (Virtual Extensible LAN) builds the tunnels that form the SD-Access overlay data plane. LISP handles the control plane in SD-Access.

questions.question: What control-plane protocol does SD-Access use?
questions.explanation: LISP (Locator/ID Separation Protocol) is the SD-Access control plane. It maps EIDs (endpoint identifiers) to RLOCs (routing locators).

questions.question: What is Cisco DNA Center (now called Catalyst Center)?
options: The SDN controller at the heart of SD-Access
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center is the SDN controller at the heart of SD-Access, and also a general network management platform.

questions.question: What is the fabric in SD-Access?
options: Overlay + underlay together — the physical and virtual network as a whole
questions.explanation: Fabric = overlay + underlay together. It is the physical and virtual network viewed as a single logical whole.

questions.question: What does intent-based networking (IBN) mean?
options: The engineer types every command manually
The engineer declares intended behavior and DNA Center translates it into device configs
The network runs with no controller at all
questions.explanation: In IBN, the engineer declares *what* the network should do (intent), and DNA Center translates that intent into actual device configurations.

questions.question: Which SD-Access node connects to devices outside the SD-Access domain (e.g., a WAN router)?
questions.explanation: Border node: connects to devices outside the SD-Access domain. Edge node connects to end hosts. Control node runs LISP for the control plane.

questions.question: In a brownfield SD-Access deployment, what does DNA Center NOT do?
questions.explanation: In a brownfield deployment (existing network), DNA Center does not configure the underlay. In a greenfield deployment, DNA Center configures the underlay optimally.

questions.question: Which routing protocol does DNA Center configure for an optimal SD-Access underlay in a greenfield deployment?
questions.explanation: DNA Center configures IS-IS for an optimal SD-Access underlay in greenfield deployments.

questions.question: In LISP, what is an EID?
options: The identity of an end host
The edge switch used to reach a host
questions.explanation: EID = Endpoint Identifier, the identity of an end host in LISP. RLOC = Routing Locator, the edge switch that can be used to reach that host.

questions.question: Which is Cisco's SDN solution for data center networks using a spine-leaf topology?
questions.explanation: Cisco ACI (Application Centric Infrastructure) is Cisco's SDN solution for data centers and uses spine-leaf. SD-Access is for campus LANs. SD-WAN is for WANs.

questions.question: In the SDN layered model, where does the controller live?
questions.explanation: The Control layer is the middle layer and contains the SDN controller. The Application layer sits above with apps/scripts; Infrastructure is the devices below.

questions.question: What is a routed access layer?
options: All links between switches are Layer 3 and access switches act as default gateway
All links are trunks carrying multiple VLANs
questions.explanation: Routed access layer: all links between switches are Layer 3 and access switches act as the default gateway for end hosts — common in SD-Access underlays.
